title: The role of the sacrum in holding early developmental trauma
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
You probably never think about your sacrum.

I didn't either. Not for decades. I knew it was there, somewhere at the base of my spine, a wedge-shaped bone tucked between my hips like a secret. But I didn't know it held memories. I didn't know it was a vault.

Here's the thing. When I started working with my own nervous system, I kept hitting a wall. I could track my triggers. I could name my parts. I could breathe and orient and ground. But something deep in my body wouldn't shift. It felt like a knot at the very bottom of my spine, a tension that had been there so long I thought it was just me.

Turns out, it wasn't just me. It was my sacrum. And it was holding something I'd never let myself feel.

Stay with me here. This isn't woo-woo anatomy. This is real, grounded science that connects the shape of your skeleton to the shape of your earliest wounds. The sacrum is not just a bone. It's a record keeper. And for those of us with early developmental trauma, it often holds the heaviest chapters.

What the sacrum actually is (and why trauma therapists care about it)

Your sacrum sits at the base of your spine, between your two pelvic bones. It's triangular, roughly the size of your hand, and it forms the back wall of your pelvis. Above it sits your lumbar spine. Below it sits your tailbone. Around it runs a web of ligaments, nerves, and connective tissue that connects everything from your diaphragm to your feet.

But here's what makes it so important for trauma recovery. The sacrum is the anchor for your spinal column. It's the structural foundation for your entire upright posture. And it's intimately connected to your autonomic nervous system through the sacral parasympathetic nerves, which regulate rest, digestion, and social engagement.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, has written extensively about how the body stores traumatic memory. In his work, the sacrum is a key player. He talks about how unresolved fight, flight, and freeze responses often get held in the deep muscles of the pelvis and lower back, with the sacrum acting as a kind of fulcrum for these incomplete survival responses.

Think about it. When a baby is born, the sacrum is actually five separate vertebrae that haven't fused yet. They fuse during childhood and adolescence, usually completing around age 25. So your sacrum is literally formed during the same years your attachment patterns are being wired. The bone and the wound grow together.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger. Levine lays out how the body holds trauma in ways the mind can't access, and the sacrum is central to that story.

Developmental trauma and the bone that remembers being helpless

Let's get specific about what "developmental trauma" means here. I'm talking about the wounds that happen before you have words. Before you have a sense of self. Before you can say "that hurt me" or "I need help" or "please stop."

These are the wounds of infancy and early childhood. The mother who couldn't attune. The father who was absent or frightening. The hospital stay that separated you from your caregiver. The neglect that left you crying alone in a crib for hours. The abuse that your tiny body couldn't escape.

Here's what happens. When a baby is in distress and no one comes, the nervous system shifts into a dorsal vagal state, what Stephen Porges calls the "shutdown" or "freeze" response. The body immobilizes. The heart rate drops. The metabolism slows. The baby goes still, conserving energy, waiting for help that might not come.

And here's the part that connects to the sacrum. That freeze response is held in the deep muscles of the pelvic floor, the psoas, and the tissues surrounding the sacrum. It's a collapse pattern. The sacrum tucks under. The tailbone curls forward. The whole pelvic bowl tightens to create a sense of containment, of holding everything in.

I felt this in my own body for years without understanding it. A chronic tightness in my lower back. A sense that my pelvis was locked, like a drawer that wouldn't open. A feeling of heaviness at my base, as if I was carrying a weight I couldn't name.

I was. It was the weight of having needed help and not gotten it. The weight of having been too small to fight or flee. The weight of having frozen, and of having stayed frozen for decades.

Janina Fisher, a trauma specialist who integrates parts work with somatic approaches, often talks about how the body holds the "youngest parts" of us. These parts don't have language. They have sensation. They have posture. They have tension patterns. And they live in the bones, especially the sacrum, which formed during the very years those parts were born.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy. Deb Dana's work translates Porges' research into clinical practice, and it's helped me understand why my sacrum felt like a locked box.

What releasing the sacrum can feel like (and why it's so hard)

I want to be honest with you. Releasing the sacrum is not a gentle process. It's not a relaxing massage or a nice stretch. It's a slow, patient, often uncomfortable excavation of very old survival patterns.

When I first started working with a somatic therapist who focused on the pelvis, I expected relief. I expected to feel lighter. Instead, I felt terror. I felt rage. I felt a grief so deep I couldn't breathe. My body started shaking, not in a gentle tremor but in a full-body convulsion that lasted for what felt like hours.

That was the freeze response finally moving. That was my sacrum letting go of a pattern it had held since infancy. And it was terrifying.

Here's why it's so hard. The sacrum is not just holding the trauma. It's holding the strategy you used to survive the trauma. That tension, that collapse, that locking down, it kept you alive. It helped you endure what you couldn't escape. And your body doesn't know the difference between a survival strategy and a threat. It just knows that if it lets go, something bad might happen.

So the sacrum holds on. It grips. It clenches. It stays frozen, even when the danger is long gone. Even when you're safe. Even when you want to let go.

This is where the work gets really personal. You have to build a relationship with this bone. You have to approach it with curiosity instead of force. You have to listen to what it's been trying to say, not try to fix it or change it or make it feel better.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice. Levine's work on how the body releases trauma is dense but deeply validating. He describes the process of pendulation, moving between the felt sense of the wound and the felt sense of safety, and that's exactly what sacrum work requires.

Practical ways to start working with your sacrum

I'm not going to give you a five-step protocol. That would be dishonest. This work is too slow and too individual for that. But I can tell you what has helped me and what I've seen help others.

First, get curious about the position of your sacrum right now. Without trying to change anything, just notice. Is your tailbone tucked under, like you're bracing for impact? Is your lower back arched, pushing your sacrum forward? Is there a sense of numbness, a deadness, in that area? Notice what just happened in your body as you read that.

Second, start to build awareness of your pelvic floor. I know, I know. It's a weird thing to think about. But the pelvic floor connects directly to the sacrum, and it's often holding the same tension. When you're sitting, can you feel the contact between your sitting bones and the chair? Can you feel the space between your pubic bone and your tailbone? Can you breathe into that space, even a little?

Third, work with a professional if you can. Somatic Experiencing practitioners, craniosacral therapists, and pelvic floor physical therapists who understand trauma are worth their weight in gold. This is not DIY territory. The sacrum holds too much for you to go at it alone.

Fourth, be patient. I mean really patient. This bone took decades to form its patterns. It's not going to release in a weekend retreat or a single session. It's going to release in tiny increments, over months and years, as your nervous system learns that it's finally safe enough to let go.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored. Deb Dana's book offers exercises for tracking and shifting your autonomic state, which is essential for doing this kind of deep body work.

I want to say one more thing. If you feel nothing when you think about your sacrum, if it's just a blank, if you can't feel anything there at all, that's okay. That numbness is information. It's a sign that your body has learned to disconnect from that area to survive. And that disconnection can be the first thing you work with, not the last.

Wild, right? The very thing that's protecting you is also the thing you need to feel.

Healing Journey Resources

  • The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy by Deb Dana - A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical for understanding your nervous system.
  • Waking the Tiger by Peter Levine - For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering for understanding how the body holds trauma.
  • In an Unspoken Voice by Peter Levine - Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing and the process of trauma release.
  • Anchored by Deb Dana - A practical guide to working with your nervous system and tracking your autonomic state.
